title: Carrot and parsnip soup
subtitle: Naturally sweet, thick, and warmly spiced
excerpt: Carrots and parsnips blended together with cumin and a little cream into a thick, golden-orange winter soup. The combination of the two sweetest British root vegetables makes a soup that is warming, inexpensive, and very easy to make.
sourceNotes: Carrot and parsnip soup is a British autumn and winter standard. The two vegetables have complementary sweetness and blend into a unified flavour that neither achieves alone. No single canonical source.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well for up to three months. Thin with stock when reheating.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles very easily. One of the most convenient soups to batch-cook.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to three days ahead. Improves overnight.

glossaryTerms.definition: Ground cumin is an earthy, slightly bitter spice that cuts through the natural sweetness of root vegetables like carrots and parsnips. A small amount ,  less than a teaspoon ,  is enough to balance sweetness without making the soup taste spiced.

content.text: Carrots and parsnips blend together into a soup that tastes like neither one alone ,  sweeter than a plain carrot soup, earthier than a plain parsnip soup, with an orange colour somewhere between the two. A small pinch of
is the seasoning that ties them together without overriding the vegetables. The recipe is completely straightforward: sweat, simmer, blend. Good bread alongside.

Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a large saucepan over a medium-low heat. Add the {{onion}} onion and cook for 6 minutes until soft. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and {{cumin-ground}} of cumin and cook for 1 minute.

Add the {{carrot}} of chopped carrots and {{parsnip}} of chopped parsnips. Pour in {{stock-vegetable}} of hot stock. Bring to a simmer and cook for 22 to 25 minutes until both vegetables are very tender.
Blend until smooth. Stir in {{double-cream}} of cream and the juice of {{lemon}} lemon. Taste and season with salt.
Ladle into bowls. Add a swirl of cream to each bowl.

items.cause: Both carrots and parsnips are sweet vegetables; together they are very sweet.
items.fix: The lemon juice is essential here ,  add it fully and taste. Add more if still too sweet. A slightly larger pinch of cumin also cuts through the sweetness.

items.cause: Large parsnips have a woody core that does not blend smoothly.
items.fix: Remove the central fibrous core from larger parsnips before cooking. Blend for at least 60 seconds. Pass through a sieve for a very smooth result.

items.cause: Parsnips are starchy and thicken considerably when blended.
items.fix: Add extra hot stock or water after blending until the consistency is right. A thick soup is fine; one that moves like porridge is too far.

items.cause: Too much cumin for the volume of vegetables.
items.fix: Three-quarters of a teaspoon is the upper limit for this quantity of vegetables. If too strong, a small extra knob of butter stirred in softens it.

content.text: Carrot and parsnip soup is a British winter standard. Both vegetables are among the cheapest available from autumn through March. Their sweetness means the soup needs very little added flavour to work. It is the kind of soup that uses up the root vegetables that pile up in most winter kitchens.
